opex: The only situations where groups live on double ko are when both white and black groups live this way, see https://senseis.xmp.net/?DoubleKo

C[Hailstones: Kochira koso (the kanji must be read ""yoro"" then) yoroshiku onegaishimasu (someone else sent me an onegaishimasu in hiragana with ""nega"" as a kanji). :D

C[Hailstones: I'm having lessons, yes. I'm not very diligent, but I feel more comfortable with the kana now. The first time I tried I found dthe first year very easy, but I didn't continue studying the hiragan and then the second year was a bit too difficult.
I've just completed the first year for the second time and am hoping that I will make a better go (sorry) of the second year in the Autumn. :)

C[Hailstones: Speaking and listening is easier than Chinese, but the reading and writing is more difficult. I've stopped studying Chinese for the moment, I found the tones and pronunciation very difficult...
opex: Hoshi stone does not aim for the corner, it's aim is fast development and influence. This move contradicts previous move, because it wants to claim corner territory. It's small, f17 better.

C[Hailstones: ""doko ni ?-n deimasu"". I assume that means where did I study. Before the economy corrected started to correct itself the University of Bristol offered evening classes. When they shut the programme down due to the Government withdrawing funding some of the teachers continued teaching their classes privately.

Hailstones: There are far more kanji (or hanzi) in Chinese, but it actually helps. In order to be able to read you really need to know every hanzi( that's Chinese for chinese character, exactly the same meaning as kanji in Japanese or hanja in Korean) in the sentence. This forces you to learn every character you come across.
Hailstones: Unfortunately you are right about the pronunciation, it can vary widely even amongst ntive speakers to the extent that they might not be able to understand each other at all...
Hailstones: The newsreader use a form of speaking that everyone can understand though.
Hailstones: Fortunately the characters usually only have one reading, though the tone may change under certain circumstances.

C[opex: Thus far all was OK, but this move feels low. I think that k4 keeps better balance of territory and influence

C[opex: I've heard some dans saying that when you see a double wing construction, you should seriously consider invading (c17). Indeed: invading at c14 provokes c15 answer and a running group. c17 gets you safe territory in sente, so that though white builds wall, you get the chance to reduce it.
C[opex: A natural response to the kick

opex: Here the placement of k16 is ideal: three-space extension from a wall of two. So the kick helps white. Without p17 there are options to enclose the corner with q18 threatening m17, or directly invade at m17. Now they are gone.
C[opex: Very shy. Yes, this move builds moyo, but it also lets white build his. More to it, white's is bigger. So go for reducing. Imagine playing tengen :)

C[opex: Interesting response. Standard are r16 to let white get a few points (r16 p18 q18 q17 r18 p16 s17 tenuki), p18 to let white live but get a peep into white's territory, or q17 to put the most pressure on white stone.
opex: Though i didnt find a way to prove that a standard response was better :))
C[opex: Why would you want to play ko here..

C[opex: Nice that you finally invaded, but usually invasions come much earlier, before yose is played. Consider this instead of m13, for example. There was much more space to run. Now even if white doesn't kill black, he may seal you in, so that you live small and don't eat enough territory to win.
